title: Ballpoint Ink on Polyester
stainChemistry: Ballpoint ink contains dye (typically crystal violet or phthalocyanine), a glycol solvent, and a resin binder. On polyester, the resin hardens as the solvent evaporates, trapping dye in a polymer matrix. The dye itself bonds through Van der Waals forces and can penetrate fiber structure. Aged ink is increasingly resistant as the resin fully polymerizes.
whyThisWorks: Alcohol (isopropyl or denatured) dissolves the resin binder on polyester, releasing the trapped dye. The freed dye transfers to a blotting cloth. Amyl acetate provides secondary solvent action for resistant resin. Working from the back pushes dissolved ink out. Multiple solvent applications are usually needed — ink releases in stages, not all at once.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply amyl acetate to dissolve the resin binder. Blot dissolved ink onto clean cloth
2. Repeat amyl acetate, blotting between applications — work from back of fabric when possible
3. Apply NSD to remove remaining residue. Flush with cool water
4. If color remains: apply H2O2 6% carefully on colorfast fabrics — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor
5. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) to neutralize. Blot dry
warnings: Never rub ink — it spreads the dye
Work from back of fabric to push stain out

steps: 1. Place a clean white cloth under the stain to catch transferred ink
2. Apply hydrogen peroxide 3% to the stain. Blot gently — do NOT rub
3. Blot dissolved ink onto the cloth. Move to clean area frequently
4. Apply Dawn dish soap and blot
5. Rinse with cold water. Air dry — no heat until stain is gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 attempts. Take to a professional — they have specialized dye-stripping agents
warnings: Never rub dye stains — rubbing spreads the color

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If the dye has penetrated the fiber (no improvement after 3 solvent passes), or if the fabric is expensive/delicate, or if dye continues to spread during treatment
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is ink ballpoint on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Is the color still actively spreading or has it dried?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
